title: What an American thinks about Turkey

transcript.text: This video is What an American Thinks About Turkey. I spent some time in Istanbul, and then I went down to see Gallipoli. I was going to go down to see Troy, but I kind of lost my motivation.
There's lots of cool stuff to see in Turkey, and it wasn't that much, but I was just kind of, when I was in Turkey, I decided to suddenly go to Nepal.
Anyways, I was in Turkey, and Istanbul, and man, Istanbul is really interesting because it's Constantinople, which is where Rome moved their capital.
They had the dual capital, and then Rome fell, and so it was just Constantinople, and so there was lots of ruins from there.

They had their Circus Maximus, which is that big oval, and it was still there, and it was more like a park you walk around, kind of more, I guess, overgrown than the one in Rome where they leave alone.
But they still had, like, the middle, big middle statue in the middle of it from back then, and right next to that, they had the underground, like, I guess it was the baths.
It was still there. It was, like, underground, and you could walk around and check it out. It was pretty cool.
Lots of cool stuff, and lots of museums there, like, there's a museum with all the ancient Roman statues and carvings and a whole lot of them.
It was pretty.
And then, but right there also, I think the two biggest mosques in the world, when the Muslims came and took over, the Turks, the Ottomans, I guess, when Constantinople fell, and what was it?
The year, they lasted all the way up until, what, like 1450 or something?

They came and built their mosques there, and, like, right next to the mosque was, like, the place where the sultan lived, and he had a great, amazing compound all within the walls and all these different, like, it was like a little mini city within these big walls, and, like, you could see all the different rooms where all of his, like, wives lived, you know?
And, like, each wife would have, like, our own apartment, and they had, like, the hangout place, which is, like, out back on the, this big, like, circular building with, like, in and inside.
It was, like, you know, this big open area, and, like, you could sell, like, I guess they had it built up, like, all these pillows everywhere where they hang out.
It's really cushy to hang out there, and I was, like, right looking over the river, and it was right next to one of the mosques, which was really intricately ornate.

With, like, paintings, and then they also had really intricate tile from back there, too, from the Constantinople days, like, 1,000 years old or something, or 1,500 years old, maybe, I don't know, but it was, like, they had one of, like, a lion and an elephant and some room, and another building from between the two mosques, I think.

It was really pretty, and they have a lot of carpet salesmen there selling carpet, and they're really aggressive, and this was, like, towards the beginning of my traveling, so I was, like, really tolerant to them, you know, I'd walk by, and they'd be, like, no, no, don't go, where are you going?

And I'd be, like, hey, and I'd come back and talk to them, and be, like, yeah, but no, sorry, I don't want to buy a carpet, and I felt kind of bad, because I'd be, like, no, but wait, no, no, get my carpet.
But there was this other guy who gave me a tour of one of the mosques, and he was a really good tour guide, but at the end, he wanted me to, he wanted, I guess it was my payment.
I didn't tip him. Maybe I should have tipped him. Maybe I did, but he wanted to show me his carpet.
He was, like, a carpet dealer, and he wanted to show me his store, and I was, like, dude, I'm not going to buy a carpet, and he got me mixed up with a different kind of traveler,
And I felt kind of bad, but I had to not go to his store, because he seemed kind of nice.
But there was this other guy who was a vendor who I saw on the Circus Maximus selling postcards, and I was, like, no, thanks.
And he was, like, where are you from, America? And he was, like, I don't like Americans. Americans are my least favorite of the tourists.
I was, like, why? And he's, like, because you're rude. You don't ever talk to me. You just walk away when I try to see stuff.
I was, like, huh. But that was just him, you know.
But then I was staying at a cool hotel, a hostel. It was, like, multi-level, and on the top level was a bar.
It was really cool, but somebody in my room was, like, really sick. I could smell that sickness, and then I went to Gallipoli,
And I got really sick for four days. Sickest I'd ever been in my life, like, hallucinating, and then I got asthma after that.
That was the day I got asthma. I was fucked up.
But I saw Gallipoli, and Gallipoli was really, oh, man, I've already done this video. I'm supposed to do, like, what I think about turkey.
Well, I'd heard a bunch of people who had, like, been drugged. Like, somebody offers them a drink, and then they wake up, and they'd passed out, and then all their stuff's stolen.
And I was, one guy wanted to buy me a coffee at a bus station. He looked kind of sleazy, and I was like, no thanks, dude. I don't want to buy me a coffee, dude.
And anyway, at that point, I decided to just not go south anymore, because I was just, like, I had just been sick, and I suddenly decided I wanted to go to Nepal.

But anyway, Gallipoli was crazy, because there's, like, two, the trenches were, like, ten feet apart, or, you know, the Australians versus the Turks, but, like, Turkey, the locals, kind of aggressive.
That was the first place. Well, I'd been to Morocco before that. They weren't as bad as Moroccans, but they were pretty aggressive as folks.
Hey, you, you, stop, no, you, you know, let me show you.
But, uh, they seemed to, uh, and one lady was accusing me of not being able to speak English, which was weird, because she couldn't understand me.

She was, she was just kind of grumpy. But they had these, like, hookah pipes where they would smoke, like, in this place we went downstairs, under the Hoek's Hostel, where they smoke, um, like, apple.
And these bongs. They put, like, flavored apple. And you don't get high at all, but I guess it was just to hang out and smoke it, taste it.
It's pretty interesting. And they had, like, other flavors, too, like a different kind of fruits or something.
But it wasn't harsh at all. But, yeah. So that's Turkey. But I heard there's other parts of Turkey that are worth it to go to.
They're really cool, like, Ephesians. Lots of ancient towns. Ancient ruins sites in the east.
And also the Noah's Ark, supposed to be in one of the, not every rat there, but I didn't check any of that out.
But, um, Istanbul was really big. Like, it took forever to get out of that town on a bus.
Um, and it's right next to a river. What looks like a river. It's really the isthmus between the Black Sea and the Mediterranean.
But it was, like, um, I didn't really see it, though. I didn't really see much of it.
It seemed like the city's, back of the city was built to it.
There were, like, buildings blocking you where you couldn't actually, there weren't, like, many walkways right to the edge to be able to look off and see it.
But maybe it was just because it wasn't adventurous enough.
But anyways, woo, that was a long one.
Good luck.
Let's save the city.
Let's save the city.
Thank you.
Let's save the city.
